feedback.text: Online games are now very well designed for FOMO (Fear of missing out). My example here is the World of Warcraft, which repeatedly offers small events so that you once again pay a month to avoid missing something. This tactic is very often used by live service games. Call of Duty uses a matchmaking system where new players come together with others who have bought many skis. If you buy skins, you get matched with weaker players to win more. After some time, then again with better to buy again and get matched with worse. This is pure psychological manipulation. The next problem is so quite all mobile games. At the outset, you can often make a great deal and a lot, but from a certain point you need to wait forever or invest huge sums to continue. Flash of Clans, where an upgrade takes one week at some point. Idle Heroes, where it is necessary to invest EUR 200 in events in order to complete it and get it ‘the new one’. There are also games where the manufacturer itself creates bot accounts and gets everything without paying, so that a ‘wall’ invests more money to keep it with. (this was in an idle game, but I no longer know the name). Moreover, the price is no longer the same as ‘initial’, where EUR 0.50 is paid. It usually starts at 1.50 and goes up to EUR 200. If parents do not stop this, young people could lose a lot of money with a few clicks. Then there are games that go offline after ‘short’ time and you ‘lost’ everything that was created. MXM (Master X Master), was online only for 6 months and I bought a ‘founding package’ of EUR 100, which was simply away. It is impossible to play the game. Sword of Legends Online is also an MMO that went offline after 1 year. If it would be possible for the manufacturer to produce at least a ‘private server’ patch in order to play ‘solely’ offline. Here is a complete page listing games taken offline: https://delistedgames.com/ It would really make sense to put an end to the entire game industry, to stop the mobile game game in order to prevent these manipulative tactics. What was also done with the Loot Boxes at that time. Unfortunately, the problem is that it became even worse and companies simply switched to worse tactics. (Battle pass, FOMO events, SBMM to spend more money, data sold in mobile apps with up to 1000 different providers)
